cases.claimant: Driver stationary at junction waiting to emerge onto main road
cases.defendant: Driver travelling along main road with nearside indicator flashing
cases.summary: The claimant was stationary at a junction with a main road. The defendant was driving slowly along the main road with their nearside indicator flashing. The claimant, relying on both the signal and the defendant's slow speed, began to manoeuvre onto the main road. The defendant drove straight on and the vehicles collided. The judge decided that the claimant was entitled to move off given the speed of the defendant's vehicle and the flashing indicator, and held the defendant liable for the collision.
key_principles: A driver who gives a misleading indication bears liability when another road user reasonably relies upon that signal.
The combination of a flashing indicator and slow speed together entitled the claimant to conclude the defendant intended to turn.

Key considerations: speed of the parties at the time of the collision, whether an indication was given and whether it was misleading, positioning of the vehicles at the point of collision, evidence from witnesses to support or contradict the indication.

cases.defendant: Driver turning right into field for motocross event
cases.outcome: 50/50 on appeal (first instance: 100% in favour of claimant)
cases.summary: The claimant was riding a motorcycle along a single carriageway with one lane in each direction (60 mph speed limit). The claimant was travelling behind the defendant whom he intended to overtake. The defendant intended to turn right into a field to attend a motocross event. As the defendant attempted to turn right, a collision occurred. At first instance the judge found in favour of the claimant fully. The defendant appealed and on appeal both parties were found equally to blame, with liability awarded on a 50/50 basis.
key_principles: The defendant was negligent for failing to indicate and failing to notice the presence of the claimant's vehicle before turning right.

The claimant was negligent for failing to notice the defendant's vehicle was slowing down and, given the claimant was aware of the motocross event on the right, should not have attempted to overtake when he did.

Key considerations: whether the turning party indicated, whether indication was given in good time, whether mirrors were checked sufficiently before commencing the turn, speed of the parties, area of damage to both vehicles, and the layout of the road.

cases.claimant: Driver entering roundabout from dual carriageway
cases.defendant: Driver who missed their exit and continued around the outside of the roundabout
cases.outcome: 50/50 on appeal (first instance: claimant held liable)
cases.summary: The claimant and defendant entered a roundabout from a dual carriageway in different lanes. The defendant missed their exit and continued around the roundabout on the outside. The claimant, anticipating the defendant leaving at the previous exit, then sought to exit the roundabout and collided with the defendant. At first instance the claimant was held liable. On appeal the court apportioned liability 50/50 as the defendant was negligent in failing to appreciate that in continuing around the roundabout they presented a possible danger to other road users.
key_principles: A driver who misses their exit on a roundabout and continues around on the outside lane creates a foreseeable danger to other road users who reasonably anticipate the outer-lane vehicle will exit.

Highway Code Rule 163: in all cases watch out for and give plenty of room to long vehicles which might have to take a different course approaching and on roundabouts. Liability should be apportioned with the long vehicle bearing the greater share of the liability.

Generally, the driver who pulls out onto a roundabout into the path of a vehicle approaching from the right is 100% liable, but excessive speed by the established driver can lead to contributory negligence of perhaps 25%.
Where both drivers are already established on a roundabout, liability is often apportioned 50/50 in the absence of evidence as to who was in the wrong lane.

Key considerations: entrance of the vehicles onto the roundabout and their intended exits, layout of the roundabout and road markings, speed of the vehicles, areas of damage to the vehicles to assist in determining which version is more probable.

cases.filename: Assessing Liabilty in Road Traffic Accidents.docx
cases.case_name: Assessing Liabilty in Road Traffic Accidents
Rai v Advantage Insurance Company Limited
cases.defendant: Advantage Insurance Company Limited (t/a Hastings Insurance)
cases.summary: Pedestrian struck by a vehicle executing a right turn into a slip road at night. The claimant and her sister were crossing the slip road wearing dark, non-reflective clothing in unlit conditions. The driver did not see them before impact. The court found that the combination of low conspicuity, an unlit background, partial A-pillar masking, and the driver's reasonable attentional focus on oncoming traffic at the initiation of the turn meant that a reasonably careful driver would not necessarily have perceived the pedestrians before they stepped into the running lane. The claimant failed to establish breach of duty or causation on the balance of probabilities. The claim was dismissed. Contributory negligence did not arise. Significant as a pre-June 2026 Highway Code amendment authority on the hierarchy of road users.
key_principles: The standard of care is that of the reasonably careful and competent driver — not a counsel of perfection, and not judged with hindsight (Birch v Paulson, Ahanonu v South East London & Kent Bus Co)
Whether an object is strictly visible is not the same as whether it would realistically be seen — the court must assess conspicuity and signal value
A-pillar masking during a turning manoeuvre is a relevant factor in assessing whether a driver could reasonably have perceived a pedestrian
A driver initiating a right turn across a live carriageway is reasonably expected to direct primary attention towards oncoming traffic, limiting perception of peripheral hazards
Pedestrian priority under Highway Code Rule 170 does not assist the claimant if the driver could not reasonably have perceived the pedestrians
A breach of the Highway Code does not of itself give rise to liability (s.38(7) Road Traffic Act 1988)
Where the evidence is inherently uncertain, evidential gaps fall against the party bearing the burden of proof
Contemporaneous police footage may carry greater weight than subsequent expert reconstruction
Statements by a driver at the scene must be interpreted in context — a remark consistent with reasonable driving behaviour is not an admission of negligence
It is rare for a pedestrian to be more responsible than a driver unless the pedestrian suddenly moves into the path of an oncoming vehicle (Eagle v Chambers)
cases.significance: High Court authority on pedestrian/vehicle liability at night — pre-June 2026 Highway Code hierarchy of road users amendment. Important benchmark for visibility, A-pillar masking, and attentional focus analysis.
key_paragraphs: §9: Mr Bolton's 'too busy' comment — 'I was too busy focusing on if cars were coming, not the path itself'
§17: Standard of care — reasonable care, not ideal care (Birch v Paulson); warning against hindsight reconstruction (Ahanonu)
§18: Highway Code Rules 170, 180, 204 and 206 — breach does not of itself give rise to liability under s.38(7) RTA 1988
§57: Claimant looked before crossing but in the wrong direction — to her right rather than to her left
§59: Police drive-through footage — pedestrian in dark clothing was 'challenging to see'
§65: Mr Loat's evidence preferred — measured, consistent with physical evidence, candid about limitations
§69-70: Low conspicuity, unlit background, A-pillar masking, and attentional focus significantly restricted perception opportunity
§71: Not satisfied on balance of probabilities that a reasonably careful driver should have perceived the claimant before she stepped into the running lane
§72: Court must avoid judging driver's conduct with hindsight or by reference to idealised standard of perfection
§77: Claimant has not established that Mr Bolton breached his duty of care — evidential uncertainties fall against the party bearing the burden of proof
